title: Swimming Calories Calculator 2026 — Crawl, | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Swimming Calories Burned by Style and Time
description: Swimming calories: calculate kcal burned swimming crawl, breaststroke, backstroke, or butterfly based on weight and duration. With official swimming MET table.
intro: Swimming is one of the **most complete sports**: works the whole body, zero joint impact, and has **very high calorie burn** due to water resistance. Burn depends on **three factors**: your weight, the style (**butterfly burns nearly double a recreational crawl**), and duration. This calculator uses official **MET values from the Ainsworth 2011 compendium** (the world reference) for each style and tells you how many kcal you burn with your weight and swim time. Useful for planning training, estimating calorie balance if you swim several times a week, or comparing swimming with other sports.
keyTakeaway: **Calories = (MET × 3.5 × weight in kg) / 200 × minutes**. Moderate crawl has **MET 8.3**; **butterfly MET 13.8** (almost 2x more burn).
useCases: Calculate kcal burned in your pool training.
Compare burn swimming vs. running or biking.
Plan a caloric deficit if you're losing weight with swimming.
See if your 40-min pool routine matches your daily burn goal.
Choose which style to work to maximize fat burn.

example.title: Example: 75 kg person swimming moderate crawl 45 min
example.result: 45 minutes of moderate crawl burn **490 kcal** — equivalent to a large pasta portion. Butterfly for the same duration: 815 kcal.

explanation:
## MET Formula (standard)

```
kcal/min = (MET × 3.5 × weight kg) / 200
total kcal = kcal/min × minutes
```

**MET (Metabolic Equivalent)**: 1 MET = resting burn (3.5 ml O2/kg/min). Swimming moderate crawl = 8.3 MET = consuming 8.3x more energy than rest.

## Swimming MET Table (Ainsworth 2011)

| Style | MET | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Slow dog paddle | 3.5 | Low effort |
| Water aerobics | 5.5 | Moderate resistance |
| Recreational 'no effort' | 6.0 | Pool leisure |
| Easy breaststroke | 5.3 | Recreational |
| Easy crawl (25-50 m in > 1:30) | 5.8 | Recreational |
| Backstroke | 7.0 | Technical moderate |
| Moderate crawl | 8.3 | Training |
| Intense crawl / competition | 10.0 | Competition |
| Intense breaststroke / competition | 10.3 | Very demanding |
| **Butterfly** | **13.8** | **Most demanding style** |

## Why Butterfly Burns So Much

1. **Both arms simultaneously** → high demand on shoulders, pecs, and lats.
2. **Hip and leg undulation** → abs and lower limbs.
3. **Technically difficult breathing** → superior cardiovascular demand.
4. **Little pause**: no long glide like in crawl.

That's why **you can only sustain butterfly for a few laps** before fatiguing. But those who master it burn more calories in 15 min than a runner in 30 min at 60% VO2max.

## Swimming vs. Other Sports (compared MET)

| Activity | MET | 45 min for 75 kg person |
|---|---|---|
| Butterfly | 13.8 | 815 kcal |
| Running at 12 km/h | 11.5 | 680 kcal |
| Intense breaststroke | 10.3 | 609 kcal |
| CrossFit | 8.0 | 473 kcal |
| Moderate crawl | 8.3 | 490 kcal |
| Recreational soccer | 7.0 | 414 kcal |
| Tennis singles | 8.0 | 473 kcal |
| Cycling 20 km/h | 8.0 | 473 kcal |
| Brisk walking | 4.3 | 254 kcal |
| Yoga | 2.5 | 148 kcal |

## Burning Fat With Swimming

### Aerobic Z2 in the Pool

Like in running, **80% of your volume should be Z2** — comfortable swimming where you can breathe easily, sustained pace. 3,000-4,000 m at Z2 burns ~400 kcal in 60 min.

### Pool HIIT

Short maximum-intensity sets + rest: **10 × 25 m sprint with 40 s rest** burn a lot due to **EPOC (post-exercise oxygen consumption)** — the body keeps burning calories 6-12 hours after.

## Swimming Advantages

1. **Zero impact**: ideal for overweight, injured, or older adults.
2. **Automatic cooling**: you don't overheat like running in the sun.
3. **Upper body work**: hard to achieve with treadmill or bike cardio.
4. **Posture and spine**: crawl and backstroke strengthen the whole posterior chain.

## Disadvantages

1. **Doesn't increase bone mass**: being impact-free, it doesn't protect against osteoporosis like running.
2. **Technique demands**: without good form, you burn energy inefficiently and tire quickly.
3. **Access**: you need a pool (not everyone has one).

faq.q: Which style burns the most calories?
faq.a: **Butterfly** with MET 13.8. Nearly doubles moderate crawl. Followed by **intense breaststroke** (MET 10.3) and **intense crawl** (MET 10.0). Least-calorie is **dog paddle** (MET 3.5) because it's very slow movement. **Tip**: if you can learn butterfly and do 5 minutes at the start of each session, you significantly multiply total burn.

faq.q: Does swimming burn more than running?
faq.a: **Depends on style and intensity**. Running at 12 km/h has MET 11.5; butterfly MET 13.8 (swimming wins). But running at 10 km/h MET 9.8 vs. moderate crawl MET 8.3 (running wins). In general, **intensity for intensity, running burns a bit more per minute**, but swimming has the advantage of **zero impact** and upper-body work.

faq.q: How much do I need to swim to lose 1 kg?
faq.a: Losing **1 kg of body fat = 7,700 kcal deficit**. Swimming moderate crawl (~490 kcal in 45 min), that's **~16 sessions of 45 min with just swimming**, assuming you don't eat more. In practice, most effective is to combine **swimming 3x/week + diet adjustment**: body loses weight in 6-8 weeks without starving.

faq.q: Why am I so hungry after swimming?
faq.a: Two reasons. **1)** Caloric burn is real (400-600 kcal in 45 min) and the body demands replenishment. **2)** The **thermoregulatory effect**: water cools your body and triggers hunger to generate heat. Strategy: have a protein-fat breakfast before (eggs, avocado, oatmeal) and post-workout fruit + protein to avoid attacking just anything.

faq.q: Can I burn fat just with water aerobics?
faq.a: Yes. **Water aerobics has MET 5.5** = 325 kcal in 45 min for a 75 kg person. Less intense than crawl, but ideal for beginners, pregnant women, people with joint issues. Consistency wins: 3-4 weekly classes for 3+ months produce noticeable changes, especially combined with diet.

faq.q: What's the optimal time per session?
faq.a: **Beginner**: 20-30 min, 2-3x/week, focused on technique. **Intermediate**: 45-60 min, 3-4x/week, with varied sets. **Advanced**: 60-90 min, 4-6x/week, with macrocycles. More than 90 min only if preparing open water or triathlon. Neuromuscular fatigue hits quickly if technique decays.

faq.q: What equipment do I need to start?
faq.a: Basics: **technical swimsuit/trunks** (lycra, polyester; no cotton), **swim cap** (required at most pools), **goggles**. Optional: **earplugs** if prone to otitis, **pullbuoy** (helps isolate arms), **paddles** (pull strength). Budget ~$60-100 to be well-equipped.

faq.q: Does open water swimming burn more calories?
faq.a: **Yes**, 15-25% more depending on conditions. In open water you spend extra energy on **currents, waves, sighting, lower water temp** (more thermoregulation). An open water marathon (10 km) at intense crawl can exceed 2,000 kcal. But be careful with safety and adequate supervision.

howToSteps.name: Select swimming style
howToSteps.text: Select swimming style from the dropdown (crawl, breaststroke, backstroke, butterfly, etc.).

howToSteps.name: Review the results
howToSteps.text: Review the results below the form.
